sentence_jp: 毎日日本語を勉強しています。
sentence_en: I study Japanese every day.
tip: 勉 means 'diligence' and 強 means 'strong' — the idea is that hard study makes you mentally strong.

sentence_jp: 大丈夫ですか？転んでしまいましたね。
sentence_en: Are you okay? You took a fall there.
tip: 大丈夫 is incredibly versatile — it can mean 'I'm fine', 'it's okay', 'no need', or even a polite refusal. Context is everything.

sentence_jp: 今日はとても気持ちのいい天気ですね。
sentence_en: The weather feels really nice today, doesn't it.
tip: 気 (spirit/energy) + 持ち (holding) = the feeling you're holding inside. 気持ちいい means something feels good physically or emotionally.

en: To do one's best / To persevere
sentence_jp: 試験に向けて頑張ります！
sentence_en: I'll do my best for the exam!
tip: One of the most culturally important words in Japanese. Telling someone 頑張って! is a powerful form of encouragement — 'give it everything you've got'.

sentence_jp: この曲を聴くと、子供の頃を思い出す。懐かしいな。
sentence_en: Hearing this song makes me remember childhood. So nostalgic.
tip: 懐かしい describes the warm, bittersweet feeling of nostalgia. English has no single word that captures it as precisely — a beautiful word to know.

en: I see / That makes sense / Indeed
sentence_jp: なるほど、だからそうなったんですね。
sentence_en: I see, so that's why it turned out that way.
tip: なるほど shows you've genuinely understood something. It's a natural conversation filler — though be careful using it with superiors, as it can sound slightly condescending in formal settings.

sentence_jp: このカフェ、雰囲気がいいですね。
sentence_en: This café has a great atmosphere.
tip: 雰囲気 is one of those words you'll hear constantly once you know it. Japanese people are very attuned to the atmosphere of a place or situation.

en: It can't be helped / Nothing to be done
sentence_jp: 雨が降ってきたけど、仕方がない。
sentence_en: It started raining, but it can't be helped.
tip: This phrase reflects a very Japanese acceptance of things outside one's control. Also heard as しょうがない. It's a philosophy as much as a phrase.

en: Good work / Thanks for your efforts
sentence_jp: お疲れ様でした！今日もよく頑張りましたね。
sentence_en: Good work today! You really gave it your all.
tip: Said at the end of work, after a long task, or when someone finishes something hard. It acknowledges effort — there's no real English equivalent.

sentence_jp: 彼女はいつも丁寧な言葉を使います。
sentence_en: She always uses polite language.
tip: 丁寧語 (teinei-go) is the polite speech register — the です/ます form you learn first. Being 丁寧 is a high compliment in Japanese culture.

sentence_jp: 洗い物をするのが面倒くさい。
sentence_en: Doing the dishes is such a bother.
tip: Extremely common in casual speech. Often shortened to めんどい by younger speakers. If someone says this about studying, encourage them with 頑張って！

sentence_jp: その言葉の意味を知らない。
sentence_en: I don't know the meaning of that word.
tip: 知る (shiru) means 'to come to know', so 知らない literally means 'I haven't come to know it'. Compare with わからない (I don't understand) — both useful but different!

sentence_jp: 今日は暇だから、一緒に映画でも見ない？
sentence_en: I'm free today, want to watch a movie together?
tip: 暇 can mean having free time (positive) or feeling bored/idle (slightly negative). Context tells you which. 暇人 (hima-jin) is a person with nothing to do.

en: As expected / Just as I thought / After all
sentence_jp: やっぱり、日本語は難しいですね。
sentence_en: As I expected, Japanese is difficult.
tip: Also heard as やはり in more formal speech. One of the most satisfying words — it confirms your instinct was right all along.

sentence_jp: 彼は素直な性格で、みんなに好かれています。
sentence_en: He has an honest, straightforward personality and is liked by everyone.
tip: 素直 describes someone who doesn't put up a front — genuine, easy to deal with, and receptive to guidance. A deeply valued trait in Japan.

sentence_jp: 本音を言えば、本当は行きたくなかった。
sentence_en: If I'm being honest, I didn't really want to go.
tip: 本音 (true sound) is contrasted with 建前 (tatemae — public face). This distinction is central to Japanese social culture. What someone says publicly vs. what they truly feel.

en: To read the room / To pick up on the atmosphere
sentence_jp: 空気が読めない人とは一緒にいるのが難しい。
sentence_en: It's hard to be around people who can't read the room.
tip: 空気 means 'air/atmosphere' and 読む means 'to read'. Reading the air — picking up on unspoken social cues — is considered an essential social skill in Japan.

sentence_jp: 夢を諦めないでください。
sentence_en: Please don't give up on your dreams.
tip: The word comes from Buddhist philosophy — 諦 relates to accepting truth. Interestingly, in older usage it meant 'to see clearly', not just 'to quit'.

sentence_jp: 日本語で話すとき、相槌を打つことが大切です。
sentence_en: When speaking Japanese, it's important to make listening sounds.
tip: Japanese conversation depends on frequent あいづち — はい, そうですね, なるほど, ええ. Staying silent while someone talks can seem rude or inattentive.

sentence_jp: 今日、初めて寿司を食べました！
sentence_en: Today I ate sushi for the first time!
tip: Don't confuse 初めて (hajimete — first time) with 始める (hajimeru — to start/begin). Same reading family, different kanji and meaning.

sentence_jp: もう少しだけ我慢してください。
sentence_en: Please hold on just a little longer.
tip: 我慢 isn't just patience — it implies stoically bearing hardship. A deeply respected quality in Japanese culture, particularly in adversity.

sentence_jp: 言葉は文化を映す鏡です。
sentence_en: Language is a mirror that reflects culture.
tip: 言葉 can mean a single word, language as a whole, or the things someone says. 言葉を選ぶ (choose one's words) is a phrase worth knowing early.

sentence_jp: 縁があれば、また会えるでしょう。
sentence_en: If fate allows, we'll meet again.
tip: 縁 refers to the mysterious thread that connects people — the idea that meetings and relationships aren't random but fated. A deeply Buddhist concept in everyday Japanese.

en: Let's eat / Thank you for this meal
sentence_jp: では、いただきます！
sentence_en: Well then, let's eat!
tip: Said before every meal. It's a humble expression of receiving — thanking the food, the cook, the farmers, and nature. There's no real English translation that covers it all.

en: Thank you for the meal (after eating)
sentence_jp: ごちそうさまでした！とても美味しかったです。
sentence_en: Thank you for the meal! It was delicious.
tip: The pair to いただきます. 馳走 (chisou) originally meant 'running around' — referring to the effort of gathering ingredients. A meal is an act of care.

sentence_jp: 公園を歩きながら木漏れ日を楽しんだ。
sentence_en: I enjoyed the dappled sunlight while walking through the park.
tip: One of Japanese's most beautiful untranslatable words. The interplay of light and shadow through tree leaves. English needs a whole phrase; Japanese has one elegant word.

sentence_jp: 秋の夕暮れは物悲しい気持ちになる。
sentence_en: Autumn dusk fills me with a melancholy feeling.
tip: 物 (mono) here means 'things in general' — a vague sadness that arises from the nature of things. Related to 物の哀れ (mono no aware), a key concept in Japanese aesthetics.

sentence_jp: 古い茶碗の傷にも侘び寂びの美しさがある。
sentence_en: There is a wabi-sabi beauty even in the cracks of an old tea bowl.
tip: Perhaps the most famous Japanese aesthetic concept. 侘び (wabi) = simplicity and asymmetry; 寂び (sabi) = beauty that comes with age and wear. Imperfection is the point.

sentence_jp: 料理が私の生きがいです。
sentence_en: Cooking is my reason for living.
tip: 生き (iki) = life, 甲斐 (gai) = worth/value. Your ikigai is the thing that gets you out of bed in the morning. Now a global concept — but it started here.

en: Forest bathing / Immersing oneself in nature
sentence_jp: 週末は山で森林浴をして、ストレスを解消した。
sentence_en: I relieved stress on the weekend with some forest bathing in the mountains.
tip: A recognized health practice in Japan — spending time in forests to reduce cortisol and improve wellbeing. 浴 (yoku) means 'bath' — you bathe in the atmosphere of the forest.

en: Restraint / Holding back out of politeness
sentence_jp: どうぞ遠慮なく食べてください。
sentence_en: Please help yourself — don't hold back.
tip: 遠慮 is the polite act of holding yourself back so as not to impose. When a host says 遠慮しないで, they're genuinely urging you to stop being so polite and just dig in.

en: To sense / To infer / To read between the lines
sentence_jp: 彼女の気持ちを察して、そっとしておいた。
sentence_en: I sensed how she was feeling and left her in peace.
tip: 察する is the ability to understand what someone is feeling without being told. Highly valued in Japan — it's the skill underlying 空気を読む (reading the room).

sentence_jp: 語学の上達は毎日の積み重ねが大切です。
sentence_en: For language improvement, daily accumulation is what matters.
tip: 積み重ね literally means 'stack of piles' — the image of progress building layer by layer. A word that applies perfectly to language learning.

sentence_jp: 会話の間が大切なこともある。
sentence_en: The pauses in a conversation can matter just as much.
tip: 間 is a foundational Japanese aesthetic and social concept — the meaningful pause, the space between things, the rest in music. Silence in Japan communicates as much as speech.

en: Once in a lifetime meeting / Treasure each moment
sentence_jp: 一期一会の精神で、毎日を大切に生きましょう。
sentence_en: Let's live each day preciously with the spirit of ichigo ichie.
tip: A four-character idiom from the tea ceremony — every gathering is unique and will never happen the same way again. Treat each meeting as a once-in-a-lifetime encounter.

sentence_jp: 七転び八起き！失敗しても諦めないで。
sentence_en: Fall seven times, rise eight — don't give up even when you fail.
tip: Japan's most famous proverb about resilience. No matter how many times you fall, you get up one more time. The spirit behind the Daruma doll.

en: To wear a cat / To hide one's true nature
sentence_jp: 彼女は先生の前では猫をかぶっています。
sentence_en: She wears a cat (acts all sweet and innocent) in front of the teacher.
tip: Literally 'to wear a cat' — pretending to be tame and harmless when you're actually not. The Japanese equivalent of 'wolf in sheep's clothing', but cuter.

reading: いしのうえにもさんねん
romaji: ishi no ue ni mo sannen
en: Three years even on a cold stone / Perseverance pays off
sentence_jp: 石の上にも三年という言葉のように、諦めずに続けましょう。
sentence_en: Like the saying 'three years even on a cold stone' — let's keep going without giving up.
tip: Sit long enough on a cold stone and it will warm up. Stick with something long enough and you'll get results. A perfect proverb for language learning.

en: Dumplings over flowers / Practical over beautiful
sentence_jp: 花見より花より団子で、食べ物目当てで来ました。
sentence_en: It's dumplings over flowers for me — I came for the food.
tip: Said of someone who prefers practical pleasures (food, comfort) over aesthetic ones (viewing cherry blossoms). The source of the famous manga/anime title.

en: The nail that sticks out gets hammered down
sentence_jp: 出る杭は打たれると言うが、それでも自分らしくいたい。
sentence_en: They say the nail that sticks out gets hammered down, but I still want to be myself.
tip: Japan's most famous cultural proverb. It describes social pressure towards conformity. Often quoted self-critically — modern Japan debates this one constantly.

en: A piece of cake / Before breakfast easy
sentence_jp: そんな計算、朝飯前だよ。
sentence_en: That calculation? Easy — before-breakfast stuff.
tip: Literally 'before morning rice' — something so simple you could do it before breakfast without effort. The Japanese equivalent of 'a piece of cake'.
